era_context: The early 12th century: the Crusader States are consolidating in the Levant following the First Crusade; the Song Dynasty rules southern China while the Jin Dynasty (Jurchen) is rising in the north; the Norman kingdoms are expanding in England and Sicily; and Western Christendom is experiencing the final stages of the Investiture Controversy.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited non-Islamic sources)
Mesoamerica and South America (no written sources)

events.title: Crusader military campaign in the Levant; expansion of the Kingdom of Jerusalem
events.description: King Baldwin I of Jerusalem continued military campaigns to expand and consolidate Crusader territory in the Levant. In 1116, Baldwin I was actively engaged in campaigns against Muslim forces, particularly in the region of Transjordan and the Dead Sea. These campaigns were part of the broader process of Crusader state-building and territorial expansion. The Crusaders were gradually establishing control over the Palestinian coast and interior regions.
key_figures: King Baldwin I of Jerusalem (Crusader leader)
events.certainty_note: The broader context of Crusader military expansion is well-established, but specific campaigns in 1116 are less precisely dated in available sources.

events.title: Pope Paschal II continues papacy; ongoing Investiture Controversy
events.description: Pope Paschal II (r. 1099–1118) was in the later years of his papacy in 1116. His reign had been marked by intense conflict with Emperor Henry V over the Investiture Controversy. In 1111, Paschal had negotiated the controversial Concordat of 1111 with Henry V, which temporarily ceded significant power to the emperor in exchange for recognition of papal authority. By 1116, the terms of this concordat were being challenged by reformist elements within the Church, and tensions between the papacy and the empire remained high.
events.certainty_note: Paschal II's papacy and the ongoing Investiture Controversy are well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources.

events.title: Song Dynasty military operations against the Jin Dynasty
events.description: The Song Dynasty, under Emperor Huizong, continued military operations against the Jin Dynasty (Jurchen) in northern China. The Jin had recently established control over Manchuria and were beginning to encroach on Song territory. These military conflicts were part of the broader process of the Jin Dynasty's rise to power in East Asia. The Song Dynasty would eventually lose significant northern territories to the Jin in the coming decades.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Song-Jin military conflict is well-established, but specific campaigns in 1116 are less precisely dated in available sources.

events.title: Norman consolidation in Sicily and southern Italy
events.region: Kingdom of Sicily and southern Italy
events.description: The Norman kingdom in Sicily and southern Italy continued to consolidate its power in 1116. The kingdom was ruled by a regency for the young Roger II, who would eventually become one of the most powerful rulers in the Mediterranean. The Normans were actively engaged in establishing their authority over the diverse populations of Sicily and southern Italy, including Greeks, Arabs, and Italians.

sources.name: Falcandus, Liber de Regno Sicilie (Book of the Kingdom of Sicily)

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Norman consolidation in Sicily is well-established. The specific events of 1116 are less precisely documented.

events.title: Anund Jacob of Sweden continues reign; internal conflicts
events.description: King Anund Jacob of Sweden (r. 1069–1119 or 1120) was in the later years of his reign in 1116. His reign was marked by efforts to strengthen royal authority and promote Christianity throughout Sweden. Anund Jacob was involved in conflicts with his brother Inge the Younger over succession and royal power. Swedish sources for this period are limited and often compiled centuries later.

sources.name: Swedish royal chronicles and later compilations
Saxo Grammaticus, Gesta Danorum (History of the Danes)

events.certainty_note: Swedish sources for the early 12th century are sparse and often compiled centuries later. The specific events of 1116 are not precisely documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The Investiture Controversy is sometimes portrayed as a clear conflict between secular and religious authority, but modern scholarship emphasizes the complexity of the dispute and the legitimate interests of both the papacy and the empire. The Concordat of 1111, while controversial, represented a pragmatic compromise that both sides found acceptable, at least temporarily. The conflict continued to simmer, but it was not as stark or ideologically pure as some earlier accounts suggested.
historiographic_note: The year 1116 is moderately well-documented in Western European sources, particularly regarding Crusader military activities and papal affairs. Chinese sources (Song and Jin dynasties) are abundant but often focused on court politics rather than military campaigns. Islamic sources provide parallel accounts of Crusader activities in the Levant. Northern European sources are sparse and often compiled centuries after the events. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia have virtually no written sources for this year.

graph_edges.note: Crusader military campaigns in 1116 contributed to the capture of Caesarea in 1117.
Paschal II's papacy ended with his death in 1118.
